data.title: Weekly Market Pulse: Tune Out The Noise
data.description:
Okay, I confess. It was my fault. I decided to take a couple of days off. I took my eye off the ball and the stock market fell a quick 2% while I was relaxing, eating too much and seeing some great art in the Holy City, Charleston, SC. I promise it won’t happen again, at least until my wife tells me where we’re going next.

It is a running joke within Alhambra that every time I go away for a few days the market takes a hit. Of course, that isn’t true even if that’s how we remember it; all kinds of things look different in the rear view mirror. We humans are the masters of selective memory. We remember ourselves as being calm through every downturn in the market during our investing career; only our subconscious knows better. It’s easy to look at some past correction or bear market or recession and wonder how in the world anyone could have missed something so obvious.

But you know it isn’t that easy in real time. When markets take a hit like last Friday, we assign meaning to it even though we have no way of knowing what all the other traders and investors were thinking, why they were selling or why they were not buying. We will worry that is the start of something bigger, that the correction or bear market is finally here. It feels like the end of last week was significant, but it always feels that way after a big down day. But, but, but last week we got weak economic data, there was a scary story getting passed around about a new coronavirus in China, and it was options expiration day and, and, and…. All that is true and yet no trends changed Friday. None. Every trend that was in place on Wednesday was still in place by the close Friday.

We did get some weak economic data last week. Housing starts, housing permits and existing home sales all fell in January. The housing market is weak isn’t exactly news if you ask me and all of those metrics are improved from where they were 3 to 6 months ago. I wish the housing market would improve but with uncertainty about future economic policy at extremes, I’m more concerned about next month and the one after that. Last week’s data didn’t change anything. More weak economic news came from the S&P Global US PMIs where the composite fell to 50.4, barely above the expansion line of 50 and the services component fell to 49.7, below 50 for the first time since early 2023. On the more positive side, the manufacturing index rose to 51.6, just barely enough to offset the negative of the services part. That could be a trend change but if so, it is one we expected and have talked about on our podcast.

We have been anticipating a ramp up in goods production since last year because the inventory overhang from COVID has been worked off. Even if goods consumption just continued to grow at the current pace, production (and imports) would have to rise to meet demand. As for services, despite the below 50 reading on the S&P PMI, the latest figures from December show a 6.7% year over year change in services consumption. That’s well above the average of 4% we saw during the 2010s. There is also a pretty good explanation of why services consumption may be starting to lag some. The Trump administration’s emphasis on tariffs as an everything tool means that lots of US businesses and individuals are buying goods today just in case the President does actually follow through. We’ve seen this in the import numbers and I suspect we’ll see it in the durable goods and personal consumption numbers this week. If goods consumption rises artificially, it shouldn’t be a surprise that services has to pull back to make room for it.

There was also good news last week, data that confirmed the upturn in manufacturing. The Empire State manufacturing survey was back in positive territory where it’s been for 5 of the last 7 months. The Philly Fed manufacturing survey was also well above zero (although down from last month’s huge jump) at 18.1 and has now been positive for 9 of the last 12 months. The only bad news in the survey was the rise in prices paid from 31.9 to 40.5.

Even without the threat of tariffs, the growth rate of goods consumption was likely to rise and the growth rate of services was likely to fall; the tariff threat just accelerated the process. Prior to COVID the growth rate of the two was similar and if one turned down so did the other but since COVID that hasn’t been true. That’s why so many market pundits got the recession call wrong. When goods consumption turned down they expected services to follow but it didn’t because services consumption was supported by COVID savings and rising incomes. COVID changed consumption patterns, which still aren’t back to normal – and now they’re being distorted by the Trump administration’s tariff agenda. Goods consumption is more volatile than services consumption and the rate of change was already turning higher before the threat of tariffs. Goods production will have to ramp up some to meet that rising demand. Meanwhile, services consumption – whose growth rate was already falling pre-Trump – is still rising at a rate well above the pre-COVID average so it isn’t surprising that it continues to moderate. All the data from last week merely confirmed trends that were already in place.

We don’t know what the consumption and durable goods reports will show next week. We don’t know what tariffs will actually be enacted and sustained, or which ones will never be imposed because a trade deal gets done. We don’t know what the corporate tax rate will be for 2025 or ensuing years. We don’t know if Congress can somehow produce a bill that reduces taxes and also reduces the deficit, although history says it is unlikely. We can never know the future, but usually, change is incremental while the current environment feels more unsettled than it has in a long time. It would be natural for people to wait and see how some of these things get resolved. It is what we warned about before the election; one party control creates more uncertainty because they try to make big changes (something that applies to either party by the way). But is that uncertainty enough to cause a recession? We don’t know. We have only markets to guide us and, as I said at the top, no trends changed last week. (see the sections below).

There is no way to know if last week’s minor selloff in stocks was the start of something bigger. We can’t know whether the economy will continue to slow or when the economic policy uncertainty will be reduced. All we can do is observe and respond when necessary. Last week’s data and market action certainly weren’t sufficient to warrant any response. One week doesn’t make a trend.

Environment

The dollar was unchanged last week and remains near the high end of the range that has persisted for the last 2+ years. If you’re just looking at technicals, I think most traders would prefer to be short. But the uncertainty of tariffs adds some additional risk that it seems a lot of traders aren’t willing to assume; speculators have built a decent sized long position in the DXY. They’ve also built large – very large – short positions in the Canadian$, the Aussie$ and the New Zealand$. There’s also a large short in the Swiss Franc vs the US buck and a pretty big one in Euro futures too. Maybe all those speculators are right, and the dollar is about to soar, but I’d be careful making any assumptions about the Trump economic team. A lot of them seem to think a weaker dollar would solve a lot of problems, but their tariff agenda is getting in the way. Maybe their bet is that tariffs will be seen as so bad, a little currency appreciation will seem like a small price to pay to avoid them. Whatever their plan, right now the dollar is just marking time.

Despite the fairly stable dollar index, gold continues to defy gravity, which is not, as I’ve said before, a good sign. People don’t buy gold when they’re confident about the future; they buy gold when they’re scared. From a technical perspective, gold is way overbought so a pullback of some kind should be expected. Which makes me wonder what might assuage people’s fears.

Bond yields were little changed last week which makes perfect sense when you have a week of balanced economic data, some weaker, some stronger. We did see the odds of a Fed rate cut in May rise last week, from 18.5% to 74% so the data was seen as weak. Odds of another cut later in the year remain low though so not a lot changed. The 10-year Treasury yield is a pretty good approximation of market expectations for nominal growth, and those expectations have risen considerably since the September Fed rate cut. They’ve only come down slightly from the peak at the end of last year.

The 2-year Treasury yield is mostly influenced by Fed policy and is essentially unchanged since the end of October. The news flow may have accelerated but it isn’t having a lot of impact on markets – yet.

TIPS yields and nominal yields together give us information about inflation expectations in markets. It is quite interesting right now because while 5 and 10 year breakevens are rising, the longer term 5 year, 5 year forward is steady as a rock. The market is looking for more inflation in the short term (5 year breakeven rising from 1.86% when the Fed cut in September to 2.61% today) but stability in the long run (5 year, 5 year forward inflation expectation is 2.23%, unchanged since the first Fed rate cut).

Markets

Not a fun week b, butot too much damage either. I’d suggest zooming out a little because the year is still off to a good start (with the exception of small caps which can’t seem to catch a break). Commodities and real estate are leading YTD against US stocks and bonds b, buthe real surprise continues to be non-US markets. It is hard, to say the least, to figure out what is motivating investors to buy non-US stocks, but if the dollar weakens more, it would be a tailwind for the trade.

Large cap value stocks continue to lead in the US markets as the rally has broadened out this year. It hasn’t, however, broadened enough to get small caps off the mat.

Bonds are off to a pretty good start this year after three straight years of losses. On the other hand, don’t want to see big returns from our bonds because that would, more than likely, coincide with a weaker economy.

Sectors

Things that were previously unloved are performing well this year. Health care, energy, materials, real estate and defensive stocks are all beating the S&P 500. Consumer staples did very well last week, up nearly 2% as the market went the other direction. Within that defensive sector, food companies had a great week with Mondelez (DLZ) and Hershey (HSY) both up over 4% after performing terribly in the last quarter of 2024. General Mills (GIS) and Kraft Heinz (KHC) were up over 3% for the week.

Economy/Market Indicators

Housing weakness last month wasn’t a susurprise,s mortgage rates had surged back above 7%. But they’re coming back down so let’s see how February looks.

Economy/Economic Data

I covered this economic data at the top, but one report not mentioned was the University of Michigan consumer sentiment poll. I don’t put much weight on it because, like a lot of survey-based “data”, it has become politically polarized. The fact that Democrats are negative about things when a Republican is in the White House is no more surprising than the opposite. But these reports do seem to still have some impact on markets, and last week’s was not that great. The headline number came in at 64.7 down from the mid-month read of 67.8 and way below last month’s 71.1. Expectations and current conditions also fell. It is mildly interesting that Republican consumer sentiment stalled in February, only matching January’s 86.7. Their view of current conditions also fell from 59.3 to 55.7. That contrasts with how Democrats sentiment changed after Biden won in 2020. By FeFebruary,oth Overall sentiment and Current Conditions were much higher than where Republican attitudes stand today. Maybe that’s just the business cycle playing out but with Republican expectations so high (106.6) disappointment seems likely, at least in the short term.

The inflation measures in the U of Michigan polls reflect the current TIPS market where short term expectations are rising while long term stays constant. For this susurvey,he one year forward expectation is for 4.3% while the 5 year expectation is for 3.5%. Both of those were up from last month but they have never been very correlated with the actual outcome, so don’t get too worked up about it. This survey is a lot smaller sample than the TIPS market.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Palantir: Insanely Overpriced With Huge Risks
data.description:
Aerospace and defense stocks have sold off following proposed cuts to defense budgets of 8% annually. One of the names that sold off is Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR). In this report, I will look beyond the generic bearish case. I will assess whether the positives, if any, are big enough to offset the bearish arguments.

The Bearish Case For Palantir

The bearish case for Palantir is simple. The company is expensive on all metrics. With a potential cut to defense spending, the growth of the company could be limited. This puts pressure on winning new contracts to support growth and may lead to existing contracts being renegotiated or not fully executed. So, the risk profile for Palantir has elevated while the fundamentals already were not attractive.

Arguments For The Bullish Case For Palantir

While cutting defense budgets in general is not a good thing, I do believe that there could be some benefits for Palantir. Next to that, we should provide a proper context to the cuts. The first thing to keep in mind is the activities of Palantir. Palantir has three software platforms: Gotham, Foundry and AIP.

Gotham is used to identify patterns in data sets and is used by governments. Foundry was established to create a central operating system for data and is used by governments and commercial customers. AIP has been established to link data to artificial intelligence.

Palantir Accounts For A Fraction Of The Defense Budget

In recent years, there has been a push to implement artificial intelligence in defense and law enforcement solutions. The defense contract monitor developed by The Aerospace Forum shows that Palantir has received $5.9 billion in defense contracts since 2018. Since 2018, the global defense budget has been north of $20 trillion. The US budget has been $6.2 trillion. That means that Palantir received just 0.1% of the US defense budget. So, there is no strong indication that the US government could make big cuts by awarding Palantir fewer contracts.

Palantir’s AIP Can Be Leveraged To Reduce Costs

In fact, I do believe that Palantir’s AI solutions could play a pivotal role to cut costs in defense and within the US government. Using AI significant amounts of intelligence data can be processed. Furthermore, AI can be used to implement predictive analysis for maintenance. Putting it simple, it means that leverage AI could result in lower sustainment costs for defense platforms.

Risk #1: Palantir Is Not A Pure US Defense Play But High Revenue Concentration

While Palantir stock has dropped on a subdued outlook for defense, the reality is that its sales are divided between commercial and government sales. The company has a total of 711 customers in 90 different industries. 55% of the sales are derived from government customers and 45% from commercial customers.

The second split we can make is between US and international sales. US sales accounted for 66% of the sales while international sales accounted for the remaining 34%. That, however, does not indicate that 66% of the sales are at risk. That is because the US sales include government sales as well as commercial sales.

Combining the sales by customer type and geographical location, we observed that 42% of the total sales are derived from US Government contracts. So, there is a risk in terms of revenue concentration. However, there could also be an opportunity assuming that data and AI leverage could be utilized to identify opportunities for cost reductions. At a time when companies and governments are pumping money into AI, not doing the same for the government and defense services is counterintuitive.

Risk #2: Palantir Has A $9.1 Billion Soft Backlog

Palantir can lean on a backlog valued at $5.4 billion. That may seem comfortable given the company’s $3 billion in sales. However, the backlog includes options which may or may not be exercised. The US Government is not allowed to exercise options one year ahead of the execution timeframe. So, there definitely is some risk to the $6.4 billion backlog. On top of that, the company has $3.7 billion in IDIQs. IDIQs are contract vehicles against which task and delivery orders can be issued. This would bring the total soft backlog to $9.1 billion.

Palantir Risks Profile Leans In The Negative Direction

The risk profile for Palantir leans to the negative direction given the overvaluation, the high customer concentration and the soft nature of the backlog. Somewhat counteracting that is the fact that Palantir contracts do not account for a huge portion of the US defense budget. Furthermore, the company’s data analytics and AI solutions may be utilized to cut costs within the US Government.

The last element we consider is the company’s valuation. To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on earnings, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I was somewhat hopeful that an EV/EBITDA analysis would provide some backing to the share price. However, the analysis has shown that this is not the case. The company is overvalued for all of the upcoming years. This is despite annual earnings growth of more than 50% and free cash flow growth of nearly 50%. The elements of Palantir that I am impressed with are the lack of bank debt and the huge free cash flow. On the flip side, the current valuation does not provide a prudent way to return value to shareholders through share repurchases. As a result, I am marking the stock a sell. In fact, a strong sell. My screener only ranges from sell to strong buy. That is the reason why a sell rating came out instead of a strong sell rating.

Conclusion: Huge Opportunities Do Not Warrant The Current Stock Price

I do believe that implementation of data analytics and AI can significantly improve efficiency and reduce costs. As a result, rather than Palantir being part of the cost problem, I believe it is part of the solution. However, after a data-driven assessment, I found that the company’s valuation does not provide any support for the current stock prices. While the company has no debt and positive free cash flow, there are also risks in the form of its valuation, softness of the backlog and customer concentration.

This may eventually be a call that will end up being wrong, but at this moment I do believe the stock is a strong sell from a fundamental perspective.

data.title: My Favorite Strategy To Trade Long-Term Treasury Bonds
data.description:
Introduction

In a diversified portfolio, government bonds are often considered a great way to diversify and hedge against crashes. However, years like 2022 show that bonds and stocks can fall together as well. The following strategy solves this problem by using signals to go long and short on bonds via Direxion Daily 20 Year Plus Treasury Bull 3X Shares (NYSEARCA:TMF) and ProShares Ultrashort 20+ Year Treasury (NYSEARCA:TBT), enabling investors to hedge their portfolios more effectively. Additionally, the strategy outperforms the market and produces positive alpha in comparison to both long-term bonds and SPY SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

The Strategy

One of the most important indicators for bonds, especially long-term bonds, are interest rates, which generally are a function of inflation. Since inflation can be seen in commodities, it is worth watching indices such as iPath Bloomberg Commodity Index Total Return ETN (DJP).

The first part of the strategy is to compare the 30-day performance of SPY to that of DJP. If DJP outperforms SPY, I open a long position in TBT to short bonds as this means that commodity prices and inflation are rising faster than asset prices.

On the other side, if SPY outperforms DJP, this means that assets are rising faster than inflation. However, as this still can mean higher inflation, DJP also has to have a negative 30-day performance to open our long position in TMF to buy long-term treasury bonds.

The last factor I consider is the trend. For this strategy, I define the trend as rather TLT (iShares 20+ Year Bond ETF) is above or below its 200-day moving average. Positions in TMF are only allowed if TLT is above the line, while positions in TBT are only allowed if TLT is below the line.

Overall, I update this signal once a week.

Performance

As can be seen in Figure 1, the strategy produces positive alpha in comparison to both SPY (stocks) and TLT (bonds). Additionally, it has a negative beta to SPY indicating that it works as a hedging strategy.

Figure 2 gives information about the nominal performance of the strategy. It outperforms both TLT and SPY in average performance and Sharpe ratio. While it has a higher volatility than TLT, its drawdown is lower than that of the base index.

Since I advertised the strategy in the beginning as a way to hedge one portfolio and as being able to give directional signals, one has to put this to the test by analyzing the strategy performance during a crash where bonds performed well (2020) and one where they performed badly (2022).

The 2020 Crash

If you look at Figure 3, you can see the strategy's performance during the first quarter of 2020 when the big crash occurred. As you can see, the strategy showed a performance of 66.5%, far better than the TLT which only yielded 22.5%, while SPY was down 23.8%. Additionally, the strategy only showed half of the TLT drawdown, while its Sharpe ratio is almost twice as high. In Figure 4, you can also see the asset allocation of the strategy during the crash with orange being cash and purple being TMF.

The 2022 Crash

While the 2020 crash was deflationary and therefore caused bonds to rise, the 2022 crash was inflationary. As the FED raised interest rates, bonds fell, leading to the normal bond hedge not working.

Figure 5 shows the performance of the strategy during the first half of 2022 when the crash occurred. The strategy rose by 41.8%, while both TLT and SPY fell by almost 20% during the same period.

Figure 6 also shows the asset allocation during this crash. Here the colors have the same meaning with red being TBT.

Risks

As with every other quantitative strategy, this one also runs the risk of the old saying: "Past performance is no indicator of future performance". However, the reasoning behind the strategy makes sense in the first place, which is why the critique is only partly true. Additionally, I created the idea for the strategy first and only backtested it afterward, which means that the risk of data mining should not be an issue. The only thing added after the original idea was the trend approach as a criterion for fewer trades.

Concerning the leveraged ETFs used in the strategy, one should consider that leveraged ETFs like TMF and TBT carry much higher risks, and are not suitable options for risk-averse investors. It is important for you to read the fund's prospectus of both TMF and TBT as well as SEC's related article to gain a full understanding of these risks

Conclusion

The presented strategy gives investors an easy approach to trading long-term bonds that beats both the bond and the stock market. It is especially useful to hedge against crashes more efficiently as it also protects investors from inflationary crashes where bonds usually fall.

data.title: i-80 Gold: Progress In Capitalization Strategy, Development Of Gold Projects In Nevada Drive Stock Recovery
data.description:
A 'Hold' Rating for Shares of i-80 Gold Corp.

This analysis recommends a “Hold” rating on the publicly traded shares of i-80 Gold Corp. (TSX:IAU:CA).

About its own profile, i-80 Gold Corp. says:

“i-80 Gold Corp. is a Nevada-focused mining company with a goal of achieving mid-tier gold producer status through the development of multiple deposits within the Company's advanced-stage property portfolio with processing at i-80's centralized milling facilities.”

This Is How It Has Been Since The Previous Coverage

We are slightly lowering the rating to Hold from Buy of the previous article as we would like to advise a more cautious stance despite the potential of this small gold producer to develop into a mid-sized gold producer in an extremely favorable environment for the gold price. In fact, in the previous article, we predicted a rise in the price of gold due to strong demand for safe haven assets. Since then, gold has risen by 70 to 72 percent. When it comes to i-80 Gold Corp instead, this stock is still lagging behind schedule, and this delay is reflected in a loss of traction of -49.12% compared to the concurrent growth of 50.10% for the S&P 500. (Since the last analysis on September 8, 2024, and at the time of this article, these are the changes in the market value of the securities.) The Fed's tight interest rate policy is a major blow to sentiment around this stock as i-80 Gold Corp. needs financing capital to grow and develop into a mid-sized gold producer. The Fed's delay in cutting interest rates due to stickier inflation than initially thought was a negative surprise for the markets, especially for the i-80 Gold Corp.’s shares, for the reasons just highlighted. In fact, as things seemed to be moving toward a gradual easing of borrowing costs, starting with the Fed's first jumbo rate cut of 50 bps on September 18, 2024, the shares of i-80 Gold Corp. were well on their way to a recovery. Then in mid-November, the hype surrounding the “Trump trade,” with his uncertain multi-proposition plan to boost US growth, dealt another blow to sentiment. Trump’s proposals, which caused renewed inflation concerns, led to several trading sessions of pullbacks in the price of gold, sliding to a monthly low. This led to a correction in the share price of i-80 Gold Corp., as i-80 Gold Corp.’s shares are correlated to the yellow metal. Then the interest rate cut on December 18, 2024, lifted morale with recovery effects that could be observed in the stock price. As a kind of confirmation that things were as we described, that is, with the Fed dampening the market sentiment due to its “higher-for-longer” stance on rate policy, could be given by the plunge of i-80 Gold Corp. shares following the announcement of the 60.6-million-unit public offering. Likely, the announcement was perceived by market participants as another sign of the temporary impracticality of the main avenues for growth capital.

But now things appear to be restoring to better times: Last month we saw important developments in the relationship between i-80 Gold Corp. and its primary lender, and that bodes well for progress in the development of its gold mining projects. There is value in this company's portfolio, but for it to be brought to the surface, financing conditions will need to become less and less restrictive. This will happen, but it will still require patience, so in our opinion, investors would be wise to be cautious and prefer to assign a buy view to other opportunities for the time being.

Nevertheless, There Is A Correlation Between Gold and i-80 Gold Corp

The idea of having i-80 Gold Corp. shares in the portfolio is to benefit from their positive correlation with changes in gold prices, and the correlation, in our view, is fueled by bullish catalysts that will be highlighted later in this analysis. Before we go any further, we want to explain what we mean by "positive correlation." It is that when the price of gold is rising, i-80 Gold's share price is likely to be dominated by bullish sentiment in the stock market. This type of relationship, which links i-80 Gold Corp.'s stock to spot gold price fluctuations, is represented by the yellow area chart that is mostly above zero in the "I-80 GOLD CORP vs. XAUUSD FXOPEN chart" below.

The Upside Catalysts Are As Follows: The Gold Price Outlook

The price of gold per ounce is still expected to move up, and Goldman Sachs recently raised its price target for gold to 2025 – the opinion of a US bank major among leading analysts. Yahoo Finance wrote:

“Gold's (GC=F) glittering run in 2025 may have more room to rise higher, Goldman Sachs believes. On Tuesday, the investment bank lifted its year-end price target for gold to $3,100 an ounce, from $2,890 previously. Goldman said "structurally higher" central bank demand will add 9% to the price of gold by the end of the year, also helped by a slight boost from ETF holdings.”

There is also something important, about the likely impact on gold prices from concerns over President Trump's fear of spiraling tariffs, highlighted by Goldman and that Yahoo Finance added as follows:

“However, if policy uncertainty — including tariff fears — stays high, higher speculative positioning for longer could push gold prices as high as $3,300 an ounce by year-end," Goldman strategist Lina Thomas wrote in a note to clients.”

This means that with the expected continued speculation driving the gold price even higher and the fact that i-80 Gold Corp.'s share price is positively correlated with the gold price, it would make no sense to put i-80 Gold Corp.'s stock on the radar for a short-selling strategy that - contrary to Goldman Sachs' vision - needs a bearish trend to be as profitable as possible.

Therefore, investors may want to hold shares of i-80 Gold Corp. – and be prepared to add to the position after a sharp decline (currently this is not the case as the stock is overbought) – while the price of gold is on an uptrend. We recommend holding the shares of i-80 Gold Corp. because there are clear and likely uncontroversial reasons to do so. But while the rise in the gold price and the upside catalysts highlighted later are very good reasons, they do not close the circle, yet, and probably represent, among other things, the easiest part of the task of laying the foundation for our recommendation. Therefore, to arrive at a balanced recommendation, we have not only outlined the arguments for the Hold recommendation but also provided some evidence that the counterarguments do not represent the right stance at this time. In fact, we also want to draw the attention of market participants to comparing a contrarian investment strategy with its success rate according to the latest available statistics. Traders considering betting against the sentiment influenced by the rising gold price with i-80 Gold Corp. will be surprised to read that short-selling strategies result in the need to build up the necessary reserves to mitigate very poor performance.

Supported by the statistics, it is generally believed that short-selling strategies are not recommended at this time. In fact, 2025 got off to a rocky start for short sellers, according to data from S3 Partners provided to Yahoo Finance. Traders “have lost $73 billion between US and Canadian markets to start 2025,” Yahoo Finance wrote, noting that traders tried to bet too heavily on contrarian trends while equity markets in general were showing a healthy uptrend by any metric, including gold and gold stocks.

More on Upside Catalysts: Income Is On Track To Get Better

The higher gold price creates a favourable environment for i-80 Gold Corp.'s earnings, a key driver of sentiment toward North American equities, to rise sharply. The company doesn't want to miss this growth opportunity to gradually improve its income lines if, given the momentum of the average realized gold price on track to move further above $2,290 per ounce in 9M 2024 (up 19% year-over-year), the operations on their side can generate more sales volume and mineralized material sales than 7,186 ounces and 29,041 tons, respectively, in 9M of 2024. Thus, both revenues of $27.1 million (down 7% year-on-year) and net income of negative $0.26 per share (worsening from the year-on-year loss of $0.13 per share) for the first nine months of 2024 are well positioned to improve, and sentiment on the stock can only gain. The company has therefore adopted a new development plan that will involve the construction of five gold mines and will transform the company into a mid-sized gold producer with annual production of 400,000 to 500,000 ounces of gold in the coming years.

More on Upside Catalysts: The Drilling Potential

The company is well positioned to achieve its growth objectives with results from over 24,400 metres of drilling indicating the potential for further development of high-grade mineralisation from at least three of the company's five most advanced gold projects. Exploration could provide additional upside to a catalyst-rich 2025, starting as early as the current quarter when the company plans to announce PEAs (“Preliminary Economic Assessments”) for all five of its most advanced gold projects: Granite Creek (combined underground and open pit), Ruby Hill Complex (combined Archimedes underground and Mineral Point open pit) and the McCoy-Cove underground project.

Update On The McCoy Cove Project

Earlier this month, i-80 Gold Corp. updated the 2025 PEA for the Cove Project, an underground exploration project located on the Battle Mountain-Eureka Trend in Northern Nevada, confirming the potential to become the first of three underground mines that will form i-80 Gold's future production base and feed high-grade material to a fully permitted central processing plant. The drilling has the potential to make Cove an even larger and longer-lived underground producer than the updated PEA indicates: 100,000 ounces of gold per year over 8 years at cash costs of $1,194 per ounce and All-In-Sustaining (“AISC”) costs of $1,303 per ounce, based on updated 311,000 ounces at 8.2 grams per tonne ("g/t") in indicated gold resources and 1.16 Moz at 8.9 g/t in inferred gold mineral resources. The company will provide a further update through a feasibility study before the end of this year. The Cove underground gold mine will produce the yellow metal cheaper than the $1,388 per ounce. We are using this price level as a benchmark to assess whether production at Cove will be cost-effective or not. In a previous article, we wrote:

“This is the average AISC per ounce of the world's gold miners, based on each operator's most recent available figures from 2024. This AISC estimate is highlighted in a report by senior geologist and industrial professor Xuan-Ce Wang at the Guangzhou Institute of Geochemistry of the Chinese Academy of Sciences."”

Based on a gold price of $2,175/oz, the after-tax net present value (“NPV” at a 5% discount rate) is $271 million or $0.62/share, giving an internal rate of return (“IRR”) of 30%. However, if the gold price increases to $2,900/oz, which is more in line with the current value of $2,931.57/oz (as of this writing), the after-tax NPV (at a 5% discount rate) increases to $582 million or $1.33/share (versus the current share price of $1.14/share of i-80 Gold Corp.) and the IRR increases to 52%. In addition to the benefit of comparing the NPV/share to the share price at the current gold price/ounce, which is expected to trade even higher, the IRR estimates also promise to improve the sentiment around i-80 Gold Corp.'s share price. Even an IRR of 30% or more would make Cove a robust gold mining project compared to other metal projects we have found for our analysis so far.

The estimated capital to build the mine is $157 million.

Update on the Archimedes Underground Project

Earlier this month, i-80 Gold Corp. updated the 2025 PEA for the Archimedes Underground Project at the southeast end of the Battle Mountain-Eureka Trend in northeast Nevada, confirming the potential to become another major pillar of future production for i-80 Gold Corp. Assuming the gold price remains robust as analysts expect, there is still room for improvement in the economics of this mine, although Archimedes is probably not the best project in the company's portfolio in terms of distance to the mill and grade of mineralization. The updated 2025 PEA assumes the Archimedes Underground Project will produce 100,000 ounces of gold annually for 10 years, but the operation is significantly less cost-effective than the Cove Project. Estimated LOM cash costs are $1,769 per ounce and AISC is $1,893 per ounce. However, drilling and technical studies can still reduce costs and extend the life of operations. The company will release a feasibility study in 2028, allowing ample time to work on “Mine Life” and smooth costs. Also, the significantly higher ounces of inferred resources that can be added by drilling compared to indicated resources provide optimism that better estimates may be forthcoming. The Archimedes Underground Project has mineral resources of 436,000 ounces at 7.6 g/t in indicated gold mineral resources and 988,000 ounces at 7.3 g/t in inferred gold mineral resources. The fact that the company has left the mineral grade of the inferred resources unchanged is a good sign in our view. When there is little or no economic viability data to support their estimates, geologists cannot draw any conclusions about resources other than inferring them. Their approach is conservative. However, as the pool of inferred mineral resources remains very large and their inferred metallic gold grade is high and consistent with higher-tier indicated resources, there is reason to hope that the company can achieve even better cost estimates and a longer mine life by developing these inferred resources.

Based on $2,175/oz, this gold price assumption results in an after-tax net present value (“NPV” at a 5% discount rate) of $127 million, or an after-tax NPV (at 5% rate) of $0.29/share, yielding an IRR of 23%. But as the spot gold price rises to $2,900/oz, the NPV becomes $1.32/share versus i-80 Gold Corp.’s share price of $1.14/share (at the time of this writing), yielding an IRR of 75%. As the gold price continues to rise, the potential of ever-improving sentiment around the share price is gaining momentum as NPV and IRR become more enticing.

To build this mine, the company needs $49 million.

"In the coming weeks, we look forward to releasing updated PEAs for Granite Creek (both open pit and underground) and the Ruby Hill Complex (Archimedes underground and Mineral Point open pit)," stated Richard Young, Chief Executive Officer of i-80 Gold.", earlier this month.

This means that the company is going to release PEAs for Granite Creek and Ruby Hill Complex (Mineral Point open pit) following McCoy-Cove and Archimedes.

More on Upside Catalysts: Metal-Rich State of Nevada and Proximity to Major Infrastructure Mitigates the Investment Risk

This portfolio of highly mineralized gold deposits is strategically located in a gold-rich state such as Nevada where the Sprott ESG Mining Risk Heat Map 2025, as for all mining regions in North America, assigns a “minimal risk” to conduct exploration and mining activities. The investment risk for the lenders, stock market participants interested in the company and all other stockholders of i-80 Gold is further mitigated by the fact that the company claims to be “the third largest resource holder in the state of Nevada.” The company also says that Nevada is one of the “largest gold producers in the world.” In addition, the company emphasizes that its complex is located near major transportation and communication routes that provide easy access to end markets in Nevada. As such, i-80 Gold Corp. is physically well-positioned to capture significant value from its “undeveloped high-grade gold mineralization.”, the company also says.

More on Upside Catalysts: Recapitalization Is Well Underway To Sustain Development Projects

The company is pursuing a recapitalization strategy to support its development projects. The recapitalization is well underway and includes the opportunity to extend capital utilization through a six-month extension of the convertible loan agreement with Orion Mine Finance from December 31, 2025, to June 30, 2026, as i-80 Gold Corp. had $239.04 million of debt as of September 30, 2024. The balance sheet showed cash and short-term investments of $21.8 million, which, together with funds raised from operations and the ability granted by Orion to defer upcoming gold and silver delivery obligations, will support the business, including covering 12-month interest expense of approximately $34.9 million as of the third quarter of 2024. The company is relying on the issuance of new shares to shareholders, board members and the open market to raise approximately $20 million (C$29 million).

The Stock Price Is Trading High, Hold It For Now and Always Look For Dips to Add Shares

Shares of i-80 Gold Corp., which trades on the TSX under the symbol IAU:CA, were at CAD$1.14/share, giving the stock a market capitalization of CAD$500.25 million at the time of writing.

The shares are trading at high price levels as they are close to pulling their head completely out of the shackles of the MA Ribbon. However, shares are also closer to the lower boundary than the upper boundary of the 52-week range of CAD$0.47/share to CAD$2.20/share.

But the 14 Relative Strength indicator of 75.52x, which shows overbought levels, says there is not much room to close the gap from here to the upper boundary of the 52-week range. This means shares will need to suffer a small setback before the recovery continues “after issuing a going concern warning” Seeking Alpha reported in mid-November 2024.

However, these concerns have been allayed as the company has reported progress in extending the term of funds with its lenders to keep the development projects going and the company is also finally issuing the PEAs.

The headwind that could pave the way for a decline in i-80 Gold Corp. stock price is investors tempering their bullishness on gold as hopes for a rate cut from the Federal Reserve fade. For now, disinflation appears to be proceeding at a slower pace, and some Fed officials even believe it's wiser to keep rates a little higher and a little longer than expected to be more certain that inflation won't cause dangerous setbacks. Also, Trump's plans (deregulation, higher corporate taxes, tougher crackdowns on illegal immigration, and increased tariffs) threaten to reignite inflation. If the price per ounce of non-income-yielding gold is hurt by fading hopes for rate cuts, shares of this stock could be poised to make lower prices.

Conclusion

This analysis recommends a “Hold” rating on the publicly traded shares of i-80 Gold Corp., a Nevada-focused mining company with the goal of achieving mid-tier gold producer status by developing multiple deposits.

Investment risk is mitigated by Nevada’s gold-rich nature and proximity to key infrastructure connected to the gold end-markets. The price of the gold metal is also rising, driven by its safe-haven properties amid a challenging global geopolitical climate and the Fed’s 100 basis point rate cut in 2024 and another cut expected in the second half of 2025.

This stock experienced a sharp crash in mid-November 2024 as markets began to sense that the company might have trouble continuing its operations in Nevada. Things have changed since then as the company has been able to agree to an extension of the loan capital it has available and is also issuing shares to the market and to its largest shareholders and directors to raise capital to sustain day-to-day operations. The negative sentiment was likely compounded by short-selling and this analysis also explains why traders should refrain from doing so for the time being. The company has also made progress as it finally issues PEAs for its key gold mine development projects. The stock price on the market recovered from the ongoing concern warning in mid-November but is now too close to overbought levels.

Investors should hold their shares for now and wait for a pullback before strengthening their position, which makes sense as recent progress with lenders and PEAs is probably fueling a positive correlation with the promising outlook for gold prices.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Marvell Q4 Preview: XPU Architecture With Integrated Co-Packaged Optics
data.description:
I assigned a ‘Strong Buy’ to Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) in December 2024, highlighting the production ramp in custom silicon. I think Marvell will disclose more information about its latest XPU architecture with integrated co-packaged optics technology in Q4. I am taking advantage of the recent stock market pull-back to buy more Marvell. I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a one-year target price of $140 per share.

XPU Architecture with Integrated Co-packaged Optics

On January 6th, 2025, Marvell announced its progress of its custom XPU architecture with co-packaged optics technology. The integrated optics allow faster connections between XPUs and distance 100 times longer than electrical cabling, as disclosed in the announcement.

As discussed in my previous articles, both Marvell and Broadcom (AVGO) are the main custom ASIC silicon providers for cloud Hyperscalers. For instance, Amazon (AMZN) has been working with Marvell for several years to develop AWS Trainium, its in-house AI chips for AI training and inference workloads. AWS Trainium2 has been adopted by Amazon and other partners. In Amazon’s recent earnings call, the management disclosed that Amazon collaborated with Anthropic to build Project Rainier, a cluster of Trainium 2 ultra servers for AI workloads. Amazon plans to launch its next-generation Trainium 3 chip later this year.

As such, I think the breakthrough is important for Marvell as their ASIC technology could continue to support Hyperscalers and AI model companies in developing their own GPU/XPU chips, providing cost-effective alternatives to Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD).

During the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Marvell noted a stronger-than-expected production ramp for its custom silicon partnership with Hyperscalers. The management expressed strong confidence in the custom silicon growth in the future. I believe Marvell’s growth will accelerate in the near future, driven by increased demands for custom silicon

Outlook & Valuation

Marvell is set to report its Q4 result on March 5th after the market close, guiding for around 26% year-over-year growth in revenue, as detailed in the table below.

Data center business represented 72.6% of total revenue and grew by 98% year-over-year in Q3 FY25. It is evident that the custom silicon and data center have become the primary growth driver for Marvell in the near future. Assuming its data center business grows by 90% in Q4, aligned with the recent growth trajectory, Marvell’s data center business would contribute around 35% growth to the topline. As such, I think Marvell’s Q4 guidance is quite conservative.

For the near-term growth, I am considering the following factors:

  • Data Center: I calculate the segment will represent more than 70% of total revenue for FY25. As custom silicon production ramp-up has just begun, I forecast Marvell’s data center business will continue growing by 50% in FY26 and FY27, contributing 35% growth to the topline.
  • Enterprise Networking: I forecast 10% revenue growth, in line with the networking and routers market growth.
  • I continue to forecast Carrier infrastructure and Consumer businesses will grow by 5% annually, aligned with historical average.
  • Putting together, I calculate Marvell will deliver 40% revenue growth in FY26 and FY27. As AI market matures, I anticipate Marvell’s revenue will decelerate to 23% from FY28, followed by 20% from FY30 and 15% from FY32.
  • Marvell has invested heavily in its R&D for custom silicon technology, and there were many up-front expenses when collaborating with cloud Hyperscalers. Notably, they spent 34.4% of total revenue in R&D in FY24. I anticipate its margin expansion will be primarily driven by R&D normalization and improvement in COGS. I model 310bps annual margin expansion, driven by 200bps from R&D normalization and 80bps from COGS improvement and 30bps from reduction in SG&A expenses.
  • The cost of equity is calculated to be 14.4%, assuming: risk-free rate 3.6%; beta 1.8; equity risk premium 6%.

I calculate the free cash flow from equity (FCFE) as follows:

Discounting all the future FCFE to the end of FY25, the one-year target price is calculated to be $140 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

I anticipate there will be some questions about tariff impact during the incoming earnings call. As China represents 43% of total revenue, any tariff or geopolitical risks could pose significant business impacts on Marvell’s business growth. As reported by the media, Marvell withdrew its China R&D team in late 2022. However, Marvell still has significant business exposure in China.

Verdict

I think Marvell is likely to provide a strong growth guidance for FY26, driven by the strong production ramp up of its custom silicon technology. I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a one-year target price of $140 per share.
